mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators looking to automate cluster lifecycle management across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of initial setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the necessary components.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This command adds the Cluster API Helm chart repository.

steps.title: Install Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API components using Helm, which includes the necessary Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and controllers.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

```

> Install Cluster API in the kube-system namespace.
commands: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Cluster API custom resource to define a new Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infra-cluster
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProviderCluster' with your actual infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infra-cluster
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Cluster API components to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Ensure all Cluster API pods are in the Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and warns about potential data loss and orphaned resources.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete cluster my-cluster

```

> This will remove the Cluster API installation.
> This will delete the custom resource and associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your cluster state. This step includes draining nodes if necessary, upgrading the components, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get clusters

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Check the status of your clusters before proceeding.
> Upgrade to the latest version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRDs'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRDs are already installed or if there are version mismatches.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl get crd' to check existing CRDs and uninstall them if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Cluster not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified cluster resource does not exist.

**Fix:**
Verify the cluster name and namespace using 'kubectl get clusters'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration in the Cluster API components.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may indicate issues with your kubeconfig or the API server.

**Fix:**
Ensure your kubeconfig is correctly set up and that the API server is running.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved error handling.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated some legacy APIs related to cluster management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your custom resources to the new API versions.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your cluster with a status of 'Ready'.
codeSnippets: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infra-cluster
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required resources.
